content.problemStatement:
You have a grid with NNN rows and MMM columns. The cell at the intersection of the iii-th row and jjj-th column contains the value Ai,jA_{i, j}Ai,j​.

Each Ai,jA_{i, j}Ai,j​ is either 000 or 111.

Let's also label the lines of the grid: there are N+1N+1N+1 horizontal segments that will be labeled 0,1,2,…,N0, 1, 2, \ldots, N0,1,2,…,N from top to bottom, and M+1M+1M+1 vertical segments that we label 0,1,2,…,M0, 1, 2, \ldots, M0,1,2,…,M from left to right.

The below images showcase the labeling for a 3×43\times 43×4 grid: horizontal on the left, vertical on the right.


  
  
Let (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) denote the intersection of horizontal line xxx and vertical line yyy.

You would like to partition the grid into 444 parts.

This will be done as follows.

First, you must start at (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) (the top-left corner of the grid), and take a path along the grid lines to reach (N,M)(N, M)(N,M) (the bottom-right corner).

Each move of this path must be taken either one step to the right or one step down, i.e. if you are standing at (x,y)(x, y)(x,y), you can move to either (x+1,y)(x+1, y)(x+1,y) or (x,y+1)(x, y+1)(x,y+1) (provided the destination cell exists.)

This path splits the cells of the grid into two parts: let's call them the "left" and "right" parts.

Next, you must start at (N,0)(N, 0)(N,0) (the bottom-left corner) and take another path along the grid lines, this time to reach (0,M)(0, M)(0,M) (the top-right corner).

Each move along this path must be either upwards or rightwards, i.e. from (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) you can move to either (x−1,y)(x-1, y)(x−1,y) or (x,y+1)(x, y+1)(x,y+1).

The second path also splits the grid into two parts: let's call them the "top" and "bottom" parts.

These two paths along the grid partition all the cells into one of four parts, depending on whether they lie in the left/right and top/bottom.

Define the weight of a part to be the sum of values of all the cells in it.

If some part contains no cells, its weight is 000.

See the sample cases below for a visual explanation.
constraints: Each character of the grid is either `0` or `1`.
The sum of N⋅MN\cdot MN⋅M over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.
content.outputFormat:
Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the dimensions of the grid.
The next NNN lines describe the grid. The iii-th of these NNN lines contains a binary string of length MMM, denoting the values Ai,1,Ai,2,…,Ai,MA_{i, 1}, A_{i, 2}, \ldots, A_{i, M}Ai,1​,Ai,2​,…,Ai,M​.




Output Format
For each test case,



If no valid pair of paths exists, print the string `"No"` (without quotes) on a single line.
Otherwise, output three lines.

The first line should contain the string `"Yes"` (without quotes).
The second line should contain a string representing the first path.

This should be a string of length N+MN+MN+M containing exactly NNN occurrences of `D` and MMM occurrences of `R`.

Here, `D` represents a downward step and `R` represents a rightward step.
The third line should contain a string representing the first path.

This should be a string of length N+MN+MN+M containing exactly NNN occurrences of `U` and MMM occurrences of `R`.

Here, `U` represents an upward step and `R` represents a rightward step.



If there are multiple solutions, any of them will be accepted.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e. the strings `NO`, `No`, `nO`, `no` will all be treated as equivalent.

sampleTestCases.output:
Yes
DRDRDRRD
UUURRRUR
No
No
Yes
DDRRRRDDR
RRUURRURU
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The first path in the sample output is DRDRDRRD, which corresponds to the following movement:
(0,0)→(1,0)→(1,1)→(2,1)→(2,2)→(3,2)→(3,3)→(3,4)→(4,4)(0, 0) \to (1, 0) \to (1, 1) \to (2, 1) \to (2, 2) \to (3, 2) \to (3, 3) \to (3, 4) \to (4, 4)(0,0)→(1,0)→(1,1)→(2,1)→(2,2)→(3,2)→(3,3)→(3,4)→(4,4)
The second path is UUURRRUR which corresponds to
(4,0)→(3,0)→(2,0)→(1,0)→(1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3)→(0,3)→(0,4)(4, 0) \to (3, 0) \to (2, 0) \to (1, 0) \to (1, 1) \to (1, 2) \to (1, 3) \to (0, 3) \to (0, 4)(4,0)→(3,0)→(2,0)→(1,0)→(1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3)→(0,3)→(0,4)
The two chosen paths in the grid can be seen below.
The red path is the first one, and the green path is the second.
The gray cells have the value 111, while the white cells have value 000.

  
  

Here is the grid with both paths together. The section where they overlap is colored blue.

The image showcases three clear sections: "top-right" with a weight of 222, "bottom-left" with a weight of 666, and "bottom-right" with a weight of 444.
The "top-left" section is empty, because there's no cell that's both to the left of the red path and above the green path. This empty section has a weight of 000, as defined in the statement.
The four weights are hence {0,2,4,6}\{0, 2, 4, 6\}{0,2,4,6}. All four are distinct, so this is a valid choice of paths.
Test cases 222 and 333: It can be shown that no valid solution exists.
Test case 444: The paths described in the sample output are visualized below. Again, gray cells denote those with value 111, while white cells denote those with value 000.
The first path is marked in red, the second path in green, and the part where they intersect is in blue.

It can be seen that the areas of the four regions are 5,1,2,35, 1, 2, 35,1,2,3 which are all distinct, as desired.
